perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will inform each of its employees, agents and affiliates (collectively, "Representatives") who will receive Confidential Information of the obligations under this Agreement and agrees to take all commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from taking any action that would constitute a breach of the terms of this Agreement. In any event, the Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will generally inform its employees, agents and affiliates (collectively, "Representatives") who may receive Confidential Information of some obligations under this Agreement and will consider taking measures to restrain its Representatives from possibly taking action that could be seen as a breach of the terms of this Agreement. The Recipient may be responsible for certain breaches of this Agreement by some of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the obligation on the Recipient to inform its representatives about the confidentiality agreement and make its responsibility for breaches by its representatives less clear. 'Generally inform', 'some obligations', 'consider taking measures', 'possibly taking action', 'could be seen as' and 'may be responsible' all introduce ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to inform representatives and lessening the responsibility for breaches, the modified text contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to prevent misappropriation of confidential information, which can be considered a trade secret under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity, weakening the recipient's obligations regarding confidential information and their representatives' actions. This contradicts the intent of CUTSA, as explained by the LLM, which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The original contract mandated informing representatives and ensuring they don't breach the agreement, whereas the modified version uses weaker language like 'generally inform', 'some obligations', and 'may be responsible', making enforcement less certain. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. The weakened language in the changed text increases the risk of such breaches, potentially undermining the 'reasonable measures' required to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: One party hereto ("Owner") may disclose to the other party hereto ("Recipient") certain Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement which Owner deems proprietary and confidential. The Parties agree that Recipient shall not use and shall prevent the disclosure of any information it receives from Owner that is marked PROPRIETARY AND CONFIDENTIAL, or similarly marked, or any other information (whether delivered in writing or verbally) which by its nature would be reasonably considered as confidential, to any other person, firm or corporation or delivered in connection with the evaluation of the Transaction, except as provided herein, and shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such information as Recipient employs with respect to its own Confidential Information of like importance.
perturbation.changed_text: One party hereto ("Owner") may disclose to the other party hereto ("Recipient") some Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement which Owner considers perhaps proprietary and confidential. The Parties suggest that Recipient should try not to use and to discourage the disclosure of information it receives from Owner that is marked PROPRIETARY AND CONFIDENTIAL, or somewhat similarly marked, or information (whether delivered in writing or verbally) that might be considered confidential, to another person, firm, or corporation or delivered in connection with the Transaction evaluation, except if provided herein, and should use care to avoid disclosure of such information in a way similar to how Recipient handles its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce several ambiguities. 'Some Confidential Information', 'perhaps proprietary', 'suggest that Recipient should try', 'discourage the disclosure', 'somewhat similarly marked', 'that might be considered confidential' and 'should use care... in a way similar to' all significantly weaken the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified text contradicts CUTSA's requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The changes introduce uncertainty about the level of protection required, potentially leading to inadequate safeguarding of trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces several ambiguities, weakening the obligation to protect confidential information. This contradicts the spirit, if not the explicit letter, of trade secret protection laws like CUTSA, which require 'reasonable measures' to maintain secrecy. While the scraped law snippet doesn't explicitly state 'reasonable measures', it defines 'misappropriation' to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, especially when the knowledge was acquired under circumstances 'giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy'. The changes significantly soften this duty, potentially failing to meet a standard of reasonable care and increasing the risk of misappropriation due to the introduced ambiguities.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Parties agrees, unless otherwise required by law, not to disclose to any other person the fact that the Confidential information has been made available to the other Party, that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Transaction between the Parties, or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof).
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Parties agrees, unless perhaps required by law, to generally not disclose to outside people the idea that Confidential information has been considered by the other Party, that there might be discussions or negotiations about the Transaction between the Parties, or some of the terms, conditions or facts with respect to it.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text uses terms like 'perhaps required by law', 'generally not disclose', 'outside people', 'the idea that confidential information has been considered', 'there might be discussions', and 'some of the terms' which introduce ambiguity and weaken the obligation to maintain confidentiality about the transaction and related information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA mandates reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The ambiguous language around non-disclosure could lead to a failure to adequately protect confidential information, which, as business negotiations, would typically qualify as a trade secret.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality, which contradicts CUTSA's mandate for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The original text states 'not to disclose', whereas the modified text uses 'generally not disclose' and 'perhaps required by law'. This introduces ambiguity around the non-disclosure of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the non-disclosure obligation, the modified text increases the risk of breaching the duty to maintain secrecy, thus contradicting CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: All written data delivered by Owner to the Recipient pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of Owner, and all such written data, and all copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to Owner upon written request, or destroyed at Owner's option.
perturbation.changed_text: Most written data delivered by Owner to the Recipient under this Agreement might be considered the property of Owner, and such written data, and copies of it, should, when practical, be returned to Owner if they ask, or perhaps destroyed at Owner's suggestion.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'Most', 'might be considered', 'when practical', 'if they ask', 'perhaps destroyed', and 'at Owner's suggestion' all introduce ambiguity. It is unclear which data is considered property, what 'practical' means in the context of returning data, if a request is necessary or if destruction can simply be a suggestion.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the return or destruction of confidential data uncertain and discretionary, the modified text could violate CUTSA's requirements for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Trade secrets must be handled with a clear process and level of security that prevents the misappropriation of confidential data. The modifications weaken data control and protection, increasing the risk of misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the return or destruction of data, potentially conflicting with the requirements of CUTSA for protecting trade secrets. The original contract mandates the return or destruction of data, while the changed version uses terms like 'might be considered,' 'should, when practical,' 'if they ask,' and 'perhaps destroyed,' which weaken the obligation. This ambiguity contrasts with the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, which emphasizes the necessity of a clear process and security level to prevent data misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper means' which could occur if the data handling is not adequately enforced due to the ambiguities introduced in the changed text. The changed text undermines the original contract's aim to protect the owner's data by creating loopholes and discretionary actions, which can be reasonably argued as not taking reasonable measures to protect data.
